title: Turns, permissions, and cost limits in the agent loop

body.concept: A 'turn' is one round trip: Claude requests tool calls → SDK executes them → results feed back. Turns repeat automatically without returning control to your code. A simple query ('list files') may take 1–2 turns; a complex task ('refactor auth + update tests') can chain dozens. The SDK provides three guardrails: permission checks (allow/deny tool calls before they run), hooks (intercept or modify calls mid-flight), and cost/token limits that halt the loop if exceeded.
body.analogy: Think of the agent loop as a self-driving car on a highway. Each turn is one lane change or maneuver — the car handles them autonomously. Permissions are the guardrails on the road, hooks are your ability to grab the wheel mid-maneuver, and cost limits are the fuel gauge that forces the car to pull over.
body.visual: Prompt → [Turn 1: tool call → execute → result] → [Turn 2: tool call → execute → result] → ... → Final answer. Guardrails: permissions gate each tool call, hooks can intercept, cost limit can halt.
